subject: Astronomy & Space
title: The First Stars: Population III
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on The First Stars: Population III.

hook.text: The first stars formed about 200 million years after the Big Bang. They were enormous—up to 300 solar masses.

buildup.text: Made of pure hydrogen and helium, they contained zero metals. Astronomers call them Population III stars.

discovery.text: They burned incredibly hot and bright, living only a few million years before exploding as supernovae.

twist.text: No Population III star has ever been directly observed. They all died billions of years ago. We infer their existence.

climax.text: JWST is searching for their faint signatures in the most distant galaxies—echoes from the universe's dawn.

punchline.text: The first stars ever. They all died long ago. We're still hunting for proof.

quiz.question: What is unique about Population III stars?
options: They contained only hydrogen and helium with no heavier elements
They were the smallest and dimmest stars ever formed
They orbited supermassive black holes at the centers of galaxies
